name: Longhouse silhouette
description: Overall silhouette of a very long, narrow rectangular dwelling whose length is three to five times its width, organized around a single continuous hearth/fire spine running down the central axis. The footprint reads as a corridor of communal life: cooking, eating, sitting and sleeping bays line up along the long sides while the smoke trench bisects the interior, vented through openings in the ridge of a low gable roof. Both short (gable) ends host doors, so the building can be traversed end-to-end, and there are no interior cross-walls breaking the long volume. This is the archetypal massing of Viking longhouses, Iroquois longhouses and Anglo-Saxon mead halls — a single linear room scaled up to shelter an entire household or clan.
placement_rules: Footprint is a single long rectangle with length:width aspect ratio between 3.0 and 5.0 — narrower reads as a hall, wider as a longhouse.
Ridge of the long gable roof runs along the long axis; leave one or more smoke-hole openings along the ridge above the central hearth.
Place a single continuous hearth/fire spine on the floor down the centerline; no interior cross-walls should interrupt the long volume.
Both short (gable) ends host a main door so the building can be traversed end-to-end.
Long side walls are unbroken by major openings — windows, if any, are small and high; sleeping/sitting bays line up along the long walls.
Single storey: wall height 4-5 blocks, total height 5-8 blocks including the gable roof.

viking.notes: Curved-keel timber walls on a low cobblestone footing; turf-or-shingle gable roof with smoke holes above the central long-fire.

medieval.notes: Anglo-Saxon mead hall variant: oak timber walls on a stone plinth, steep dark-wood gable roof, twin gable-end doors.

rustic.notes: All-timber clan longhouse with weathered spruce throughout; shallow gable pitch, smoke vents along the ridge.
